pages.text:
1. CharCoat – Fire Rated Cable Coating

Purpose

To prevent flame spread and heat propagation along power, control, and instrumentation cables.

Key Technical Points

Intumescent coating: expands under fire to form an insulating char layer

Protects cable sheath integrity for a defined duration

Applied on installed cables (new or retrofit)

Effective for horizontal & vertical trays, risers, tunnels

Typical Use

Cable trays in industrial plants, substations, data centres

Areas where circuit integrity and asset protection are critical

2. CharCoat – Fire Mortar

Purpose

Fire sealing of large service penetrations through walls and floors.

Key Technical Points

Cementitious, fire-rated mortar

Provides fire resistance + mechanical strength

Suitable where openings are large or irregular

Works with stone wool backing for depth control

Typical Use

Multi-cable penetrations

High-load areas where seal must withstand impact or vibration

3. CharCoat – Fire Panels (Firestop Boards)

Purpose

Modular fire sealing where future cable additions are expected.

Key Technical Points

Fire-rated boards with sealed joints

Can be cut, removed, and reinstated

Combined with fire sealant at interfaces

Cleaner and faster than mortar in many cases

Typical Use

Data centres, commercial buildings

Shaft walls, floors with frequent service changes

4. CharCoat – Intumescent Sealant (IS Sealant)

Purpose

Fire sealing of linear joints and small penetrations.

Key Technical Points

Intumescent: expands to seal gaps during fire

Accommodates movement and thermal expansion

Used at interfaces (pipe–wall, cable–panel, joints)

Typical Use

Cable penetrations (small OD)

Pipe annular gaps

Construction joints

5. CharCoat – Structural Steel Fire Protection Coating

Purpose

To maintain load-bearing capacity of steel during fire.

Key Technical Points

Intumescent coating for steel sections

Expands to form thermal insulation layer

Fire rating depends on section factor & DFT

Applied on beams, columns, trusses

Typical Use

Exposed steel in buildings and industrial structures

Areas where passive fire resistance is mandated

One-Line Technical Positioning (For Suhas to Remember)

“Each CharCoat product addresses a specific fire risk—cables, openings, joints, or steel—but performance depends on correct system selection and execution at interfaces.”

CharCoat Passive Fire Protection – Consultant-Level Technical Overview

1. Fire Rated Cable Coating (CharCoat CC Series)

Function Limits flame spread, heat release, and secondary fire propagation along electrical cables.

System Principle

Intumescent / ablative coating

Forms a thermally insulating char layer under fire exposure

Protects cable outer sheath and delays ignition of adjacent cables

Typical Standards & Test References

IEC 60332-3 – Flame propagation of bunched cables (system performance relevance)

IEC 60331 – Circuit integrity (where applicable, cable dependent)

ASTM E84 – Surface burning characteristics (FSI / SDI)

BS 6853 : Smoke Prodcution, Smoke Density

FM 3971-1992 : 5 different tests (Derating, Salt water reaction, Burning )

BS 476 - Fire propagation index

Fire Performance Objective

Control flame spread on cable trays and risers

Reduce fire growth rate and asset damage

Improve evacuation and firefighting window

Typical Applications

Vertical & horizontal cable trays

Substations, data centres, tunnels, process plants

2. Fire Mortar (CharCoat FM Series)

Function Fire sealing of large and complex service penetrations through fire-rated walls and floors.

System Principle

Cementitious fire-resistant mortar

Often used with mineral / stone wool backing

Provides fire integrity, insulation, and mechanical stability

Typical Standards & Test References

EN 1366-3 – Fire resistance tests for service penetrations

BS 476 Part 20 & 22 – Fire resistance of building elements

EN 13501-2 – Fire resistance classification (EI ratings)

Fire Performance Objective

Maintain compartmentation

Prevent fire and hot gas passage for rated duration

Typical Fire Ratings

EI 60 / EI 90 / EI 120 (system dependent)

Typical Applications

Multi-cable and mixed service penetrations

Heavy-duty industrial walls and slabs

3. Fire Panels / Firestop Boards (CharCoat FP Series)

Function Modular fire sealing where frequent service changes are expected.

System Principle

Fire-rated boards fixed within openings

Interfaces sealed using intumescent / fire-rated sealants

Allows re-entry without structural damage

Typical Standards & Test References

EN 1366-3 – Penetration sealing systems

EN 13501-2 – Fire resistance classification

BS 476 – System fire testing

Fire Performance Objective

Maintain compartmentation with future flexibility

Faster installation and cleaner detailing compared to mortar

Typical Fire Ratings

EI 60 / EI 120 (depending on system depth and configuration)

Typical Applications

Data centres

Commercial buildings

Shafts and technical floors

4. Intumescent Sealant (CharCoat IS Series)

Function Fire sealing of linear joints and small penetrations with movement accommodation.

System Principle

Elastomeric intumescent material

Expands under fire to seal gaps

Accommodates thermal and structural movement

Typical Standards & Test References

EN 1366-3 – Penetrations

EN 1366-4 – Linear joint seals

EN 13501-2 – Fire resistance classification

Fire Performance Objective

Seal gaps during fire exposure

Maintain integrity at interfaces, which are typical failure points

Typical Applications

Cable penetrations (small OD)

Pipe annular gaps

Construction and movement joints

5. Structural Steel Fire Protection Coating (CharCoat SC Series)

Function Preserves load-bearing capacity of steel during fire.

System Principle

Intumescent coating expands to form a thick insulating char

Delays steel temperature rise beyond critical limits (~550°C)

Typical Standards & Test References

EN 13381-8 – Fire protection to structural steel

BS 476 Part 21 – Load-bearing fire resistance

EN 13501-2 – Fire resistance classification

Design Considerations

Section factor (Hp/A)

Required fire resistance period

Dry Film Thickness (DFT)

Typical Fire Ratings

R 30 / R 60 / R 90 / R 120 (section-dependent)

Typical Applications

Beams, columns, trusses

Exposed steel in buildings and industrial plants

Consultant-Level Positioning Statement (Very Important)

“Passive Fire Protection performance depends on tested systems, not individual products. Fire ratings are valid only when configuration, substrate, depth, and interfaces match tested assemblies.”

Common Consultant Questions – Suggested Responses

Q: Is this compliant with NBC 2016? ✔ NBC mandates fire resistance; testing standards define how compliance is demonstrated.

Q: EN vs IS standards – which is acceptable? ✔ EN 1366 / EN 13381 are widely accepted international fire test standards and commonly referenced in Indian projects where IS standards are limited or not system-specific.

Q: Can different products be mixed? ✔ Only if tested together as a system or supported by engineering assessment.

1. Is fire rating valid for all cable types?

Answer: Fire performance depends on cable construction, tray configuration, spacing, and coating thickness. Cable coating systems are qualified for defined cable types and installation configurations. Any deviation requires either a matching test configuration or an engineering assessment.

2. Can firestop systems be installed after services are already in place?

Answer: Yes. Firestop systems are designed for post-installation sealing and retrofit scenarios. However, access, cleaning, surface preparation, and depth control must be ensured to maintain tested performance.

3. Is fire mortar mandatory for all large openings?

Answer: No. Fire mortar is one option. Boards, modular panels, or composite systems may be more suitable depending on:

Opening size

Future service changes

Structural and vibration considerations

Selection should be risk- and usage-driven, not default-based.

4. How is movement accommodated in firestop systems?

Answer: Movement is addressed using intumescent and elastomeric sealants tested under EN 1366-4 (linear joint) conditions. Rigid systems like mortar must be combined with movement-capable interfaces where required.

5. Can the same firestop detail be used for walls and floors?

Answer: Not always. Fire performance depends on orientation, gravity effects, backing support, and installation method. Wall and floor penetrations often require separate tested configurations.

6. How do you ensure workmanship quality on site?

Answer: Through:

Approved method statements

Installer training and supervision

Stage-wise inspection checklists

Photo documentation linked to penetration IDs

Firestop is as much a process control issue as a material issue.

7. Are firestop systems maintenance-free?

Answer: Firestop systems require periodic inspection, especially after:

New cable additions

Mechanical damage

Core-cutting or retrofit work

Integrity must be maintained for the life of the building.

8. What documentation should be handed over at project closeout?

Answer: A complete firestop dossier typically includes:

Approved drawings and details

Product data sheets & test reports

Installation records

Inspection and as-built photographs

This supports future audits and renewals.

9. Can fire ratings be upgraded later if requirements change?

Answer: In some cases, yes—by:

Increasing system depth

Adding supplementary layers

Replacing interfaces

However, upgrades must be validated against tested limits.

10. How are mixed services (cables + pipes) handled in one opening?

Answer: Mixed penetrations require tested composite systems. Independent treatment of individual services does not guarantee system performance unless tested together.

11. Is third-party inspection required?

Answer: While not always mandated, third-party inspection or consultant sign-off is strongly recommended for:

High-risk buildings

Data centres

Infrastructure and industrial projects

It improves audit defensibility.

12. How do firestop systems behave during fire-fighting (hose stream)?

Answer: Robust systems are designed to withstand thermal shock and mechanical impact during firefighting. Systems tested to EN / BS standards include post-fire integrity evaluation.

13. What are the most common reasons for firestop failure during audits?

Answer:

Incorrect system selection

Incomplete depth or backing

Poor interface sealing

Unauthorised modifications after installation

Most failures are execution-related, not product-related.

14. How early should firestop be considered in the project lifecycle?

Answer: Ideally at design and coordination stage, alongside MEP routing and shaft planning. Late-stage firestop design often leads to compromised solutions.

15. Can engineering judgement replace testing?

Answer: Engineering judgement can supplement testing, but cannot override mandatory test evidence. It must be issued by a competent authority and remain within tested boundaries.

Consultant Closing Line (Useful at Stall)

“Fire performance is proven in the test furnace—but preserved only through correct detailing, execution, and control on site.”

NBC 2016 Mapping – Passive Fire Protection (Consultant Reference)

1. Is fire rating valid for all cable types?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3 (Structural & Compartmentation Requirements)

NBC Intent: Fire resistance must be based on actual installed conditions and materials forming part of the fire compartment.

Consultant Positioning: NBC does not generalise ratings across cable types. Performance must reflect actual configuration tested.

2. Can firestop systems be installed after services are already in place?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 7 (Fire Safety of Services)

NBC Intent: Fire separation must be maintained wherever services pass through fire-rated elements, irrespective of installation sequence.

Consultant Positioning: NBC permits post-installation sealing, provided fire integrity is restored.

3. Is fire mortar mandatory for all large openings?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3.4 (Fire Barriers & Compartmentation)

NBC Intent: NBC mandates fire stopping, not a specific product or material.

Consultant Positioning: Mortar is one solution; boards or composite systems are equally acceptable if performance is proven.

4. How is movement accommodated in firestop systems?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3.4.6 (Joints & Openings)

NBC Intent: Fire barriers must remain effective under expected movement conditions.

Consultant Positioning: Movement-capable systems (tested joint seals) are required where differential movement exists.

5. Can the same firestop detail be used for walls and floors?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3 (Fire Resistance of Elements)

NBC Intent: Fire resistance depends on orientation and construction of the element.

Consultant Positioning: NBC does not allow blanket replication; element-specific detailing is expected.

6. How do you ensure workmanship quality on site?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 2 (Administration), Section 7 + Part 4 (General Compliance)

NBC Intent: Fire safety provisions must be installed, inspected, and maintained as part of building compliance.

Consultant Positioning: Documentation, inspection, and supervision are implicit NBC requirements.

7. Are firestop systems maintenance-free?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 7.1 (Maintenance of Fire Protection Systems)

NBC Intent: Fire safety features must be maintained in working condition throughout building life.

Consultant Positioning: Firestopping is not “fit and forget”; integrity must be preserved.

8. What documentation is required at project closeout?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 2, Section 7 (Completion & Occupancy)

NBC Intent: Authorities may require records demonstrating compliance before occupancy.

Consultant Positioning: Firestop dossiers support Occupancy Certificate and future audits.

9. Can fire ratings be upgraded later if requirements change?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3 (Fire Resistance Requirements)

NBC Intent: Fire resistance must meet the current occupancy and risk classification.

Consultant Positioning: Upgrades are allowed if revised systems achieve required performance.

10. How are mixed services handled in one opening?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 7.6 (Service Penetrations)

NBC Intent: Penetrations must not reduce fire resistance of separating elements.

Consultant Positioning: Mixed services must be treated as one tested system, not individually.

11. Is third-party inspection required?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 2 (Administration) + Local Fire Authority Rules

NBC Intent: NBC allows authorities to demand independent verification where risk is high.

Consultant Positioning: Third-party inspection strengthens compliance defensibility.

12. How do firestop systems behave during firefighting (hose stream)?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 16 (Fire Fighting Provisions)

NBC Intent: Fire barriers must withstand firefighting actions without premature failure.

Consultant Positioning: Systems tested to EN / BS inherently consider post-fire integrity.

13. Common reasons for firestop failure during audits?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, General Compliance Requirements

NBC Intent: Fire safety measures must function as installed and intended.

Consultant Positioning: NBC failures usually arise from execution deviations, not material choice.

14. When should firestop be considered in project lifecycle?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 7 (Integration of Services)

NBC Intent: Fire safety must be integrated with building services planning.

Consultant Positioning: Early coordination is implied, even if not explicitly stated.

15. Can engineering judgement replace testing?

NBC Reference: NBC 2016 – Part 4, Section 3 + Part 2 (Approval Authority Powers)

NBC Intent: Authorities may accept alternate solutions supported by evidence.

Consultant Positioning: Engineering judgement may supplement—but not replace—test evidence.

Consultant-Safe Closing Statement

“NBC defines the fire safety intent. Tested systems demonstrate compliance. Correct execution preserves performance.”

**Fire Officer–Specific Questions & Model Answers

(Passive Fire Protection | NBC 2016 Aligned)**

1. How do you ensure fire compartmentation is not compromised by service penetrations?

Model Answer: All service penetrations through fire-rated walls and slabs are sealed using tested firestop systems that restore the same fire resistance rating as the parent element, in line with NBC 2016 Part 4 – Compartmentation requirements.

2. Are the firestop systems tested, or are they site-assembled solutions?

Model Answer: The systems are tested as complete assemblies under recognised fire test standards. Site installation strictly follows tested configurations—including substrate, depth, and interfaces—without ad-hoc modifications.

3. How do you verify that the installed system matches the tested system?

Model Answer: Verification is done through:

Approved shop drawings and details

Method statements

Stage-wise inspection and photo documentation This ensures installed work matches tested assemblies.

4. How do you address mixed penetrations (cables and pipes in one opening)?

Model Answer: Mixed services are treated as one composite firestop system, not as individual elements. Only configurations tested for mixed penetrations or supported by engineering assessment are adopted.

5. How do you ensure integrity during firefighting operations (hose stream)?

Model Answer: The firestop systems are designed to maintain post-fire integrity, including resistance to thermal shock and mechanical impact encountered during firefighting, as part of international fire test protocols.

6. How is future cable addition or removal handled without compromising fire safety?

Model Answer: Where future changes are anticipated, modular firestop panels or boards are used. After any modification, the firestop system is reinstated and inspected to maintain compartmentation.

7. Are firestop systems considered permanent, or do they require maintenance?

Model Answer: Firestop systems are passive systems, but they require periodic inspection, especially after MEP modifications, in accordance with NBC 2016 maintenance intent.

8. How do you ensure workmanship quality across different contractors?

Model Answer: Execution is controlled through:

Approved applicators

Defined installation procedures

Site supervision and inspection records This ensures consistency irrespective of trade interface.

9. How are joints and movement accommodated in fire-rated constructions?

Model Answer: Movement-prone joints are sealed using tested linear joint firestop systems capable of accommodating expected movement while maintaining fire resistance.

10. How do you ensure fire resistance ratings are not reduced during retrofit or brownfield works?

Model Answer: Any retrofit or new penetration is treated as a fresh firestop installation, fully compliant with the original fire rating and documented accordingly.

11. What documents are submitted to the Fire Department at completion?

Model Answer: A comprehensive dossier is provided, including:

Product test reports

Approved drawings

Installation records

As-built photographs This supports inspection and audit requirements.

12. Are the firestop systems aligned with NBC 2016, even if tested to EN/BS standards?

Model Answer: Yes. NBC specifies performance intent, not test methods. EN / BS standards are internationally recognised methods used to demonstrate compliance with NBC requirements.

13. How do you prevent unauthorised modifications post-handover?

Model Answer: Firestop locations are clearly identified and recorded. Any subsequent modification is subject to controlled reinstatement and inspection.

14. What are the most common non-compliances observed during fire audits?

Model Answer: Typically:

Incomplete firestop depth

Poor interface sealing

Unauthorised service additions These are addressed through strict inspection and documentation.

15. Can engineering judgement be used where testing is not available?

Model Answer: Engineering judgement may be used only within tested boundaries and must be supported by documented evidence and authority approval.

Fire Officer–Safe Closing Statement

“Our objective is to restore and preserve fire compartmentation exactly as required by NBC 2016, supported by tested systems and controlled execution.”

**Passive Fire Protection – Do & Don’t for Site Engineers

(Fire Officer’s Lens | NBC 2016 Intent)**

✅ DO – What Fire Officers Expect to See

Design & Planning

Do verify the required fire rating of each wall/slab before sealing any penetration.

Do use tested firestop systems that match the opening type, services, and orientation (wall vs floor).

Do coordinate early with MEP trades to avoid last-minute core cutting.

Execution

Do follow the approved method statement and tested configuration (depth, backing, interfaces).

Do ensure proper surface preparation (clean, dry, oil-free) before application.

Do maintain minimum required seal depth and correct backing (e.g., stone wool density/thickness).

Do seal all interfaces completely—cable-to-sealant, pipe-to-sealant, board joints.

Inspection & Documentation

Do carry out stage-wise inspections before closing shafts or ceilings.

Do label or tag firestop locations for easy identification.

Do maintain photo records (before / during / after) mapped to each penetration.

Do reinstate firestopping immediately after any modification.

Handover & Maintenance

Do submit a firestop dossier at completion (drawings, test reports, photos).

Do brief maintenance teams that firestops must not be disturbed without reinstatement.

❌ DON’T – Common Reasons for Audit Failure

Design & Coordination

Don’t treat firestopping as a finishing activity.

Don’t assume one detail works for all services and openings.

Don’t allow unplanned penetrations without defined firestop details.

Execution

Don’t use PU foam, cement mortar, or ad-hoc fillers in fire-rated elements.

Don’t leave voids, gaps, or exposed backing.

Don’t mix products from different systems unless tested together.

Don’t reduce seal depth due to access constraints.

Inspection

Don’t close shafts, false ceilings, or risers without inspection sign-off.

Don’t rely on visual appearance—measure depth and coverage.

Post-Installation

Don’t allow cables/pipes to be added later without reinstating firestops.

Don’t paint over or damage firestop seals without approval.

Don’t assume “approved earlier” means approved forever—changes void compliance.
